reasoning:
The 30-day average SOFR tracks a 30-day rolling average of daily SOFR rates. Over the past 3 weeks to March 13, 2026, the 30-day average has trended slightly upward from 3.665 to 3.672, indicating that the daily SOFR rates currently entering the rolling window are marginally higher than those dropping out from a month prior (mid-February). Furthermore, mid-March is historically associated with mild upward pressure on repo rates due to the U.S. corporate tax deadline (March 16), which tightens liquidity. Historical seasonality shown in FRED data (1/1 prior year) also points to an increase over this specific week.

The primary downside risk is the March 17-18 FOMC meeting. A rate cut would immediately lower daily SOFR for the remaining days of the observation window, mathematically guaranteeing a lower 30-day average. However, markets reflect a 92%+ probability that the Fed will hold rates steady, as inflation and labor data have stabilized. Ruling out a cut, the baseline statistical trend, recent upward momentum, and mid-month tax date effects make an increase significantly more likely than a decrease. Adjusting for the minor chance of an unexpected cut and general day-to-day noise, I estimate a 71% probability of an increase.
